sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=6).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 25, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2 has 5 green crosses. Top panel: 4 crosses at row 16.5, cols 14.5, 20.5, 32.5, 44.5. Bottom panel: 1 cross at (52.5, 44.5). Yellow/orange cell at (34.5, 32.5) - possibly a target.
- Goal model: Reduce to 1 cross using jumps.
- Action model: Same as Level 1 - click cross → direction → click target.
- Plan: First, try jumping the leftmost cross RIGHT over the adjacent cross.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 10 of about 81 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After clicking the purple figure, the crosses are back to GREEN (N color)! The purple figure disappeared and the game state reverted. This suggests:
1. The purple figure appeared when I made a "wrong" move (clicking the g marker at an invalid position)
2. Clicking the purple figure "resets" or "undoes" the mistake
3. The game is back to the original state with 5 green crosses
Looking at the current image, all crosses are green again and the purple figure is gone. This is great - I can now try the correct approach.
Let me reconsider the mechanics:
- Click a cross to select it (shows G ring)
- g markers appear showing valid jump targets
- Click the g marker to execute the jump (the cross moves to the target, jumping over an adjacent cross)
- The jumped-over cross is removed
The previous attempt failed because I clicked at (16, 8) which was the g marker position, but maybe the click wasn't precise enough or the g marker wasn't at the right position.
Let me try again more carefully. I'll select a cross and then click exactly on the g marker center.
Actually wait - looking at the previous output, when I selected the cross at (16.5, 20.5), the g markers appeared at (16.5, 8.5) showing a LEFT jump target. But col 8.5 is the leftmost cell of the top panel. The cross at 20.5 would jump LEFT over the cross at 14.5 to land at 8.5. This seems valid.
But when I clicked at (16, 8), the crosses all turned gray and a purple figure appeared. Maybe the click position was wrong - I clicked at col 8 but the g marker center was at col 8.5, so I should have clicked at col 9 or 8.
Let me try again with more precise coordinates. The g marker was at center (16.5, 8.5), so I should click at row 16, col 9 (rounding up from 8.5).
Wait, actually I realize the issue might be different. Let me re-examine: the g marker showed a target at (16.5, 8.5). But looking at the grid, the cells are at columns: 8.5, 14.5, 20.5, 26.5, 32.5, 38.5, 44.5. So col 8.5 is the leftmost cell. The cross at 20.5 jumping LEFT over 14.5 would land at 8.5. This should be a valid jump.
But maybe the game requires clicking the g marker more precisely, or maybe the mechanic is different. Let me try clicking the g marker at the exact center position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The crosses are back to green! Clicking the purple figure reset the state.
# Let me check the current state and try again with precise coordinates.

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green crosses: {len(green)}")
for n in green:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}")

# Select the cross at (16.5, 20.5) and check g marker position precisely
predict(expect_change=True, note='Select cross at 20.5')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 20}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter select:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'g']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# The g marker center is at (16.5, 8.5). Let me click at row=16, col=9 (rounding 8.5)
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click g marker at exact center')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 9}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green2 = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"\nGreen crosses after: {len(green2)}")
for n in green2:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}")

# Check for gray crosses
gray = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
print(f"Gray crosses: {len(gray)}")
for n in gray:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
